---
title: "Foundations of R & RStudio"
subtitle: "DMgtAsia · Session-I"
format:
  live-html:
    toc: true
  live-revealjs:
    output-file: slides-session1-foundations.html
    theme: [default, dmgt.scss]
    logo: dmgt_asia_logo.png
    footer: "DMgtAsia · Introduction to R & RStudio"
    width: 1280
    height: 720
    slide-number: c/t
    show-slide-number: all
    progress: true
    transition: fade
    scrollable: true
    incremental: false
webr:
  packages:
    - ggplot2
engine: knitr
include-in-header:
  - text: |
      <script src="coi-serviceworker.js"></script>
other-links:
  - text: "View as slides"
    href: slides-session1-foundations.html
    icon: easel2
---

## Data science for public health

-   Public health runs on data: surveys, surveillance, registers, trials.
-   Data science is the workflow that turns that data into decisions:
    import, tidy, transform, visualise, model, communicate.
-   We do it in a way others can check and repeat.

::: aside
The live R cells on this page run in your browser. Nothing to install.
:::

---

## Why R and RStudio

-   **R** is a language built by and for statisticians and epidemiologists.
-   It is free, open, and has a package for almost every method in public health.
-   **RStudio** is the workbench that makes R comfortable to use.
-   Everything is scripted, so an analysis is a document you can re-run, not a set of clicks.

---

## The RStudio tour

-   **Source** (top left): your scripts and Quarto documents.
-   **Console** (bottom left): R runs here.
-   **Environment** (top right): the objects you have created.
-   **Files / Plots / Help** (bottom right): outputs and documentation.
-   **Projects** keep one analysis self-contained; with `here()`, file paths just work.

---

## Objects and assignment (live)

Press **Run**. `<-` stores a value in a named object.

```{webr}
age <- 27
sex <- "female"
age
sex
```

The keyboard shortcut for `<-` is Alt/Option and minus.

---

## Quiz: assignment

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof q1 = Inputs.radio(
  ["= =", "<-", "==", "->>"],
  { label: "Which operator assigns a value to an object in R?" }
);
html`${q1 == null ? "" : q1 === "<-" ? "<strong>Correct.</strong> R assigns with <code>&lt;-</code> (Alt/Option and minus)." : "<em>Not quite.</em> R's assignment arrow points left."}`
```

---

## Vectors (live)

A vector holds several values of the same type. `c()` combines them.

```{webr}
weights <- c(2.5, 3.1, 2.9, 3.4, 2.2)   # birth weights in kg
weights
mean(weights)
length(weights)
```

Try changing a number and running again.

---

## Data types

-   **numeric** `3.4`, counts and measurements.
-   **character** `"female"`, text in quotes.
-   **logical** `TRUE` / `FALSE`.
-   **factor** a category with fixed levels (for example sex, district).

```{webr}
class(weights)
class("female")
class(weights > 3)
```

---

## Functions

-   A function takes inputs and returns an output: `mean(weights)`.
-   You name the function, then pass arguments in brackets.
-   `summary()` describes a whole vector at once.

```{webr}
summary(weights)
```

---

## Packages

-   A package is a bundle of functions. Install once, load each session.
-   `install.packages("tidyverse")` downloads it (done on your machine).
-   `library(tidyverse)` loads it for use.

```{webr}
library(ggplot2)
df <- data.frame(weight = c(2.5, 3.1, 2.9, 3.4, 2.2, 3.8))
ggplot(df, aes(weight)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 6) +
  theme_minimal()
```

---

## Quiz: install vs load

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
viewof q2 = Inputs.radio(
  ["Installs a package from the internet", "Loads an installed package for use", "Deletes a package", "Updates R"],
  { label: "What does library(tidyverse) do?" }
);
html`${q2 == null ? "" : q2 === "Loads an installed package for use" ? "<strong>Correct.</strong> install.packages() downloads once; library() loads it each session." : "<em>Try again.</em> Downloading the package is a separate step."}`
```

## Recap

-   Data science is a workflow: import, tidy, transform, visualise, communicate, reproducibly.
-   R is the language, RStudio is the workbench, Projects plus `here()` keep paths sane.
-   Objects (`<-`), vectors (`c()`), data types, and functions are the building blocks.
-   Packages add capability: install once, `library()` each session.
-   Next: best practices, then working with real data.
